did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically VGG16 and DenseNet201 architectures, to classify skin cancer types using the Skin Cancer dataset and the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset. The study evaluates these deep learning models for automated skin cancer detection and classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from applying and comparing Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, DenseNet201, etc.) on specific datasets (Skin Cancer dataset and Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset) to achieve classification accuracy. It includes a 'Materials and Methods' section detailing data augmentation and model training, and a 'Results and Discussion' section with specific performance metrics (e.g., 98% accuracy for VGG16, 90.32% for DenseNet201) and confusion matrices. Although it contains a 'Related Work' section, its primary purpose is reporting new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Leveraging Convolutional Neural Networks for Accurate Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2025 5th International Conference on Expert Clouds and Applications (ICOECA)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the effectiveness of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for automated skin cancer classification using two distinct datasets: a Kaggle Skin Cancer dataset and a Kaggle Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset. The researchers assessed multiple architectures, including VGG16, DenseNet201, ResNet50, and EfficientNet, to determine their performance in distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions. For the Skin Cancer dataset, the VGG16 architecture achieved the highest accuracy of 98%, demonstrating robust feature extraction capabilities. On the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset, DenseNet201 outperformed other models with an accuracy of 90.32%, highlighting its efficiency in handling complex patterns. The results indicate that CNNs, particularly VGG16 and DenseNet201, offer high reliability for early skin cancer detection and can aid dermatologists in making more accurate diagnoses.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified in the 'Skin Cancer dataset' (Dataset 1) and the 'Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset' (Dataset 2). In Dataset 1, benign lesions were categorized into a specific folder and included in the binary classification task. In Dataset 2, benign cases were distinguished from malignant ones, with DenseNet201 achieving an AUC of 0.96 for this class.

Classified in the 'Skin Cancer dataset' (Dataset 1) and the 'Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset' (Dataset 2). In Dataset 1, malignant lesions were categorized into a specific folder. In Dataset 2, malignant cases were the primary focus of the melanoma classification task.

Specifically studied in the second dataset ('Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset'), which contains 13,900 high-resolution images curated to advance dermatology and computer-aided diagnostic tools for this aggressive form of skin cancer. DenseNet201 was identified as the most effective architecture for this specific classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer types (Benign vs Malignant) and Melanoma detection
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'accurate skin cancer classification' using CNNs. It performs binary classification tasks: distinguishing between benign and malignant skin lesions in the 'Skin Cancer dataset' (Dataset 1) and detecting melanoma in the 'Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset' (Dataset 2). Performance is measured using accuracy, confusion matrices, and AUC (Area Under the Curve), which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer Dataset (Dataset 1)
datasets_used.description: A real-world dataset acquired via Kaggle [16], systematically categorized into two distinct classes: benign and malignant skin lesions. Organized into 'Malignant' and 'Benign' folders.

use.training: Used for training CNN architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNet B0, Inception V3, MobileNet V2). Data augmentation was applied.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation. VGG16 achieved 98% accuracy. Confusion matrix showed 47/47 malignant cases and 6/7 benign cases correctly diagnosed (1 benign misclassified as malignant).

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset (Dataset 2)
datasets_used.description: A real-world dataset sourced from Kaggle [17], containing 13,900 high-resolution images curated to advance dermatology and computer-aided diagnostic tools. Images were uniformly resized to 224 x 224 pixels.

use.training: Used for training CNN architectures (VGG16, DenseNet201, EfficientNet B3, ResNet50, Xception, Inception V3, MobileNet V2). Data augmentation was applied.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation. DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy of 90.32% (0.903). VGG16 achieved 81% accuracy. Other models ranged from 84% to 89%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, confusion matrices, and AUC (Area Under the Curve) without statistical significance testing.

Skin Cancer Dataset (Dataset 1).notes: VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy. Confusion matrix analysis for VGG16 showed 47/47 malignant cases and 6/7 benign cases correctly diagnosed (1 benign misclassified as malignant). ROC curve for VGG16 showed an AUC of 0.97 for both benign and malignant classes.

Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset (Dataset 2.notes: DenseNet201 outperformed other architectures with the highest accuracy. ROC curve for DenseNet201 showed an AUC of 0.96, indicating excellent performance in differentiating between malignant and non-malignant lesions.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically VGG16, DenseNet201, ResNet50, EfficientNet B0/B3, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, Xception, DenseNet121, and U-Net.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was employed; models were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 224 pixels for the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset; 128x128 pixels for the Skin Cancer Dataset (Dataset 1).

architecture_details.VGG16: Developed by the Visual Geometry Group; known for robust feature extraction.
architecture_details.DenseNet201: Noted for connectivity and efficient parameter usage; deep connectivity useful for detecting malignant lesions.
architecture_details.ResNet50: Contains 50 layers including fully connected, pooling, and convolutional layers; utilizes residual connections to address vanishing gradient issues.
architecture_details.EfficientNet B0: Employs compound scaling method modifying depth, width, and resolution; uses Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolutions (MBConv) and depthwise separable convolutions; has 5.3 million parameters.
architecture_details.MobileNet V2: Lightweight CNN with inverted residual structure and linear bottleneck layers.
architecture_details.U-Net: U-shaped structure with encoder (downsampling) and decoder (upsampling) paths; uses skip connections for precise segmentation.
architecture_details.Xception: Extends Inception model with depthwise separable convolutions; composed of blocks filtering input channels separately.
architecture_details.Inception V3: Uses 1x1 convolutions to reduce dimensionality and computational cost; employs factorized convolutions.

training_details.optimizer: Adam and RMSprop optimizers were mentioned in the context of related work [10], but specific optimizer for this study's models is not explicitly detailed in the provided text chunks, though standard CNN training practices are implied.

data_augmentation.rotation: Up to 30 degrees for InceptionV3, 20 degrees for others.
data_augmentation.shifts: Width and height shifts up to 30% or 20%.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Modified Falcon Finch deep convolutional neural network
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images in the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset were uniformly resized to 224 x 224 pixels; input size for the Skin Cancer Dataset was 128x128 pixels.

Data Augmentation: Applied to enhance training dataset diversity, including rotation (up to 30 degrees for InceptionV3, 20 degrees for others), width and height shifts (up to 30% or 20%), shear and zoom transformations (up to 30% or 20%), horizontal and vertical flips, and brightness adjustments.
Fill Mode: Used 'nearest' fill mode to handle new pixel values introduced by shifts or rotations.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Up to 30 degrees for InceptionV3 model; up to 20 degrees for other models.
Applied to simulate different orientations and viewpoints.
Applied to simulate different lighting conditions.
Used 'nearest' fill mode to handle new pixel values introduced by shifts or rotations.

conclusions: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) demonstrate high reliability and potential for automating skin cancer detection and classification, aiding dermatologists in making faster and more accurate diagnoses.
VGG16 was identified as the optimal architecture for the Skin Cancer dataset, achieving the highest accuracy of 98% and demonstrating robust feature extraction capabilities.

DenseNet201 was the most effective architecture for the Melanoma Cancer Image Dataset, achieving an accuracy of 90.32%, with its deep connectivity and efficient parameter usage proving well-suited for detecting malignant lesions.

The VGG16 model exhibited excellent sensitivity for malignant cases on the Skin Cancer dataset, correctly identifying 47 out of 47 malignant cases with zero false negatives, though it misclassified one benign case as malignant.

The DenseNet201 model demonstrated excellent discriminatory ability on the Melanoma dataset, achieving an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.96 and maintaining a low false positive rate while achieving a high true positive rate.

Other tested architectures, including ResNet50, EfficientNet B0/B3, Inception V3, MobileNet V2, and Xception, showed varying levels of performance, with some yielding lower accuracies (e.g., 81-89%) compared to the top performers on their respective datasets.

The study addresses a research gap regarding the limited application of CNN models specifically on the Skin Cancer and Skin Melanoma datasets, providing confidence that CNNs are a viable choice for automated skin disease detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on two datasets sourced from Kaggle, which may introduce selection bias and lacks external validation on independent clinical cohorts.
Dataset 1 (Skin Cancer) has a very small testing set (13 samples: 7 benign, 6 malignant), making the reported 98% accuracy for VGG16 statistically fragile and prone to overfitting.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare model performances; conclusions are based solely on point estimates of accuracy.
The paper does not report the training/validation/test split ratios or the total number of images in the Kaggle datasets, limiting reproducibility.
Data leakage risks are present as the paper does not explicitly confirm that data augmentation was applied only to the training set and not to the validation or test sets.
The claim of 'zero false negatives' for malignant cases in Dataset 1 is based on a tiny sample size (n=6 malignant test cases), which is not generalizable.
The study addresses a claimed research gap regarding limited CNN work on these specific datasets, but the comparison is limited to standard architectures without novel architectural contributions.
Deployment concerns are not addressed; the study focuses on offline classification accuracy without discussing computational efficiency, inference time, or integration into clinical workflows.
